corpus.title: Physical status, symptoms and health-related quality of life during a severe exacerbation of COPD: Recovery and discriminative capacity for future events.
corpus.abstract: Severe acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (AECOPD) can have a negative impact on functional capacity, symptoms and health-related quality of life (HRQOL). This study aimed to i) investigate the recovery of muscle strength, functional capacity, symptoms, and HRQOL in patients after a severe AECOPD; ii) compare with matched patients with stable COPD (SCOPD); and iii) assess whether these assessments at hospital discharge could discriminate patients' risk for future events. This observational study assessed patients with AECOPD during hospital discharge (T1) and one month after discharge (T2). Patients with SCOPD were assessed once. Quadriceps force, handgrip strength, short physical performance battery (SPPB), 6-min walk distance (6 MWD), COPD assessment test (CAT), London chest activity of daily living (LCADL), modified medical research council, checklist individual strength-fatigue, patient health questionnaire, and physical activity (Actigraph) were measured. Exacerbation-related readmission and mortality within six months and 1-year were collected. Forty-four patients with AECOPD were matched with 44 patients with SCOPD. At T2, a significant improvement was found for the SPPB total score, 6 MWD, CAT score, and LCADL score. Compared to patients with SCOPD, a worse LCADL score was found at T2 in patients with AECOPD. Patients with AECOPD that were readmitted or died had a worse SPPB classification and five-repetition sit-to-stand test at T1. Patients after severe AECOPD improved in functional capacity and HRQOL one month after hospital discharge, but ADL performance was still worse compared to SCOPD. Patients who were readmitted or died had significantly worse scores on functional tests at hospital discharge.

This observational study assessed patients with AECOPD during hospital discharge (T1) and one month after discharge (T2). Patients with SCOPD were assessed once. Quadriceps force, handgrip strength, short physical performance battery (SPPB), 6-min walk distance (6 MWD), COPD assessment test (CAT), London chest activity of daily living (LCADL), modified medical research council, checklist individual strength-fatigue, patient health questionnaire, and physical activity (Actigraph) were measured. Exacerbation-related readmission and mortality within six months and 1-year were collected. Forty-four patients with AECOPD were matched with 44 patients with SCOPD. At T2, a significant improvement was found for the SPPB total score, 6 MWD, CAT score, and LCADL score. Compared to patients with SCOPD, a worse LCADL score was found at T2 in patients with AECOPD. Patients with AECOPD that were readmitted or died had a worse SPPB classification and five-repetition sit-to-stand test at T1. Patients after severe AECOPD improved in functional capacity and HRQOL one month after hospital discharge, but ADL performance was still worse compared to SCOPD. Patients who were readmitted or died had significantly worse scores on functional tests at hospital discharge.

corpus.title: Effects of Pedometer-Based Step-Feedback on Physical Activity of Severe COPD Patients.
corpus.journal: International journal of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease
corpus.abstract: This study investigated whether adding step-feedback (step-FB) from a pedometer to pulmonary rehabilitation (PR) programs could increase the physical activity (PA) of low-activity patients with severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). We included low-activity patients with severe COPD (step-FB group: 14 patients; control group: 17 patients) who underwent PR for the first time. The usual PR program for patients with severe COPD consisted of two 8-week sessions (PR session 1: PR1, PR session 2: PR2). The step-FB group was provided a program with step-FB added to PR2 (PR2+step-FB). Furthermore, all patients were evaluated at pre-intervention (baseline), PR1, and PR2. The primary outcome of this study was the number of daily steps (steps) and energy expenditure from activity (energy expenditure), as measured by a pedometer. The secondary outcomes were dyspnea and exercise tolerance. In PR1, dyspnea, exercise tolerance, steps, and energy expenditure were significantly improved as compared to baseline, in both groups. During PR2, dyspnea and exercise tolerance were significantly improved as compared to PR1, in both groups. Steps and energy expenditure were significantly improved in the step-FB group, but not in the control group. PR improved PA by improving physical function in severe COPD patients. Adding step-FB improved PA in severe COPD patients by presenting an activity goal for improving PA. Therefore, pedometer-based step-FB is a viable addition to PR and has the potential to improve PA continuously in these patients.

corpus.title: Pulmonary Rehabilitation Exercise Based on Wearable Device Pedometer Improved Lung Cancer Patients with Impaired Pulmonary Function.
corpus.journal: Alternative therapies in health and medicine
corpus.abstract: Lung cancer patients mostly had different degrees of impaired pulmonary function, and these damage also significantly affect quality of life. The concept of pulmonary rehabilitation applicable to patients with chronic respiratory diseases is also applicable to patients with lung cancer. The current application of pulmonary rehabilitation for lung cancer is inconsistent, and reliable guidelines are lacking. The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of pulmonary rehabilitation exercise based on wearable device pedometer on lung cancer patients with impaired pulmonary function, and to find a suitable pulmonary rehabilitation program for patients with lung cancer. In this retrospective study, 100 lung cancer patients with impaired pulmonary function were included. Among them, 51 patients received pulmonary rehabilitation exercise based on a wearable device pedometer (Experiemental group), while 49 received routine nursing mode (Control group). The respiratory function, quality of life, and sports endurance of the two groups were observed. The incidence of postoperative atelectasis, pulmonary infection, hypoxemia, postoperative oxygen therapy time, chest tube indwelling time, and postoperative hospital stay in the experimental group were significantly lower than those in the control group (P < .05); The FEV1, FVC and FVE1% of the experimental group were significantly higher than those of the control group after intervention (all P < .05). Pulmonary rehabilitation exercise based on a wearable device pedometer can effectively improve the respiratory function and exercise endurance of lung cancer patients with impaired pulmonary function and can improve the quality of life and reduce the length of hospital stay.

corpus.title: Cognitive behavioural therapy combined with physical activity behavioural modification strategies during pulmonary rehabilitation in patients with COPD.
corpus.abstract: Patients with COPD who exhibit elevated levels of anxiety and/or depression are typically less able to improve symptoms and physical activity levels following a programme of pulmonary rehabilitation (PR). The objective of the present study was to provide proof of concept that offering an intervention comprising cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) alongside physical activity behavioural modification strategies (BPA) during PR is more effective in improving physical activity outcomes compared to PR and CBT alone. 32 patients with COPD (mean±sd forced expiratory volume in 1 s 42±14% predicted) were assigned 1:1 to receive PR+CBT+BPA or PR+CBT. BPA comprised motivational interviews, step-count monitoring, feedback using a pedometer and goal setting. Assessments included accelerometer-derived steps per day, movement intensity, 6-min walk distance (6MWD) and Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) scores. The magnitude of improvement across physical activity outcomes was greater for the PR+CBT+BPA compared to the PR+CBT intervention (by 829 steps per day (p=0.029) and by 80±39 vector magnitude units (p=0.042), respectively). Compared to PR and CBT alone, the PR+CBT+BPA intervention induced greater clinically meaningful improvements in HADS anxiety scores (by -2 units, 95% CI -4-1 units) and 6MWD (by 33±20 m). Providing anxious and/or depressed patients with COPD with a combined intervention of CBT and BPA during PR presents more favourable improvements in physical activity outcome measures compared to CBT alone during PR.

corpus.title: Effect of Data Reduction Techniques on Daily Moderate to Vigorous Physical Activity Collected with ActiGraph® in People with COPD.
corpus.abstract: ActiGraph® is a valid, frequently used, accelerometer to quantify moderate to vigorous physical activities (MVPA) in people with COPD. The impact of ActiGraph processing techniques on this population is unknown. This study aimed to explore the effect of data reduction techniques on MVPA in people with COPD. MVPA/day, through ActiGraph GT3X+, was estimated using: Troiano, Freedson 98 and FreedsonVM3 cutoffs, 15-s and 60-s epochs, and normal and low-frequency extension (LFE) filters. Cutoff, epoch, and filter effects were explored with Aligned Rank Transform-ANOVA. Lin's concordance correlation coefficients and Bland-Altman plots were used to evaluate agreement and bias between different techniques. The analysis included 136 people with COPD (79% male; 68 ± 8 years; FEV1 51 ± 17% predicted). MVPA/day differed according to cutoff, filter, and epoch selection (p-value < 0.001). FreedsonVM3 cutoff, 15-s epochs, and LFE yielded the highest MVPA (45 min/day, 68% of physically active participants). Troiano cutoff, 60-s epochs, and normal filter yielded the lowest MVPA (8 min/day, 20% of physically active participants). Only comparisons between Troiano and Freedson98 cutoffs presented an almost perfect agreement. ActiGraph data reduction techniques affected MVPA/day estimates and their interpretation at the individual and group level. Studies using different processing criteria should not be compared in people with COPD. Future studies with a gold standard are required to ascertain which processing technique produces the most accurate MVPA estimates in COPD. Meanwhile, future trials employing the ActiGraph GT3X+ may consider estimating MVPA based on Freedson VM3 cutofffs, 60-s epochs, and normal filter.

corpus.title: Physical Activity Profiles among Patients Admitted with Acute Exacerbations of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease.
corpus.abstract: People with hospitalised acute exacerbations of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (AECOPD) exhibit low levels of physical activity (PA) and increased risks of future exacerbations. While methods to objectively measure and express PA are established for people with stable COPD, less clarity exists for people during AECOPD. Further, the relationship between PA during AECOPD and clinically relevant outcomes remains uncertain. The purpose of the study was to evaluate how much PA (step count and intensity) people accumulate during hospitalised AECOPDs, and the effect of accumulated inpatient PA (expressed via differing metrics) on length of stay (LOS), PA recovery, and readmission risk. This study was a secondary analysis of prospective observational cohort data collected with Actigraph wActiSleep-BT devices from patients with AECOPD in a Melbourne hospital from 2016 to 2018. Step counts and PA intensity throughout the hospital admission and at one-month follow-up were collected and analysed. Sixty-eight participants were recruited for inpatient measurement, and 51 were retained for follow-up. There were no significant changes in step count or intensity across the inpatient days, but 33/51 (65%) of participants demonstrated a clinically meaningful improvement in steps per day from 3817.0 to 6173.7 at follow-up. Participants spent most time sedentary and in light PA, with both PA metrics demonstrating significant influences on LOS and follow-up PA intensity, but with generally low explanatory power (R2 value range 7-22%). Those who had LOS < 5 days spent significantly less time sedentary and more time in light PA than those with LOS ≥ 5 days (p < 0.001 for both). Time spent sedentary or in light PA appears to be the most promising metric to associate with clinically relevant outcomes related to recovery following AECOPD. These findings can inform future clinical practice for the evaluation of inpatient PA to better establish its role in the clinical management of patients with AECOPD.

devices.limitations: Estimates highly sensitive to filter (normal vs. low-frequency extension), epoch length, and cut-point choice, producing large discrepancies in MVPA minutes/day across processing methods (PMID:37629381, PMID:38394032); cross-study comparability is compromised without harmonized processing SOP. Used extensively as a secondary/exploratory outcome measure across many RCTs (NCT02161393, NCT04139200, NCT05233137, PMID:41843815, PMID:41773531, PMID:38971337).

MVPA is not natively output by activPAL software; requires derived methods (METs, step-rate thresholds, vector magnitude) with only moderate concordance against ActiGraph MVPA (CCC 0.20-0.53 depending on comparator cut-point) and no method was equivalent when benchmarked against Evenson cut-points (PMID:42419359). Group-level agreement only; individual-level validity unconfirmed.

measures_supported: gait/walking speed and cadence (digital mobility outcomes)
devices.limitations: Large multi-site validation exists for gait/mobility DMOs and MVPA-adjacent measures (549 COPD participants, 7 European sites) but repeated-measures/change-score data specific to MVPA (rather than gait speed) is less established; primarily used in UK Biobank large-cohort epidemiological analyses rather than interventional trials as a primary/secondary trial endpoint.

Multi-center use validated in large COPD cohorts (n=408, n=404) with mortality-prediction capability; used to derive a novel composite PA score outperforming step count for mortality prediction, but this novel score itself is not yet widely externally replicated beyond originating group.

Only single cross-sectional/prospective study identified in this corpus (n=96) using consumer-grade device to dichotomize patients by MVPA threshold; no repeated-measures/change-score or trial-endpoint data found; algorithm/validation methodology against reference standard not detailed in the corpus.

Only source is a single industry-sponsored completed trial (NCT05655832, N=77) designed to correlate multi-parameter sensor data (including physical activity) with exacerbations and CAT score; results not yet reported in this corpus; single sponsor authorship raises bias flag pending publication.

measures_supported: predicted 6MWD (exercise capacity proxy, not direct MVPA)
devices.limitations: Single small cross-sectional study (n=66); measures functional capacity (6MWD proxy), not MVPA directly, so relevance to this specific COI is indirect/analogous; no longitudinal or trial-endpoint data.

measures_supported: gait/walking detection and duration (not MVPA directly)
devices.limitations: COPD is only one of five mixed disease cohorts (total labeled N=83, COPD subset smaller); measures gait detection/walking duration, an adjacent construct to MVPA rather than MVPA itself; algorithm-only validation, not a standalone commercial device.
Single small feasibility study (N=25); consumer-grade device with 'very active minutes' as MVPA proxy; correlational analysis only against FEV1, no direct MVPA gold-standard comparison.

Used only as one of several reference comparators (energy expenditure) in a single small walking-trail validation study (n=20); not evaluated as a primary MVPA measurement device in its own right within this corpus.

gaps.description: MVPA estimates vary substantially (up to ~7500 steps/day and multi-fold differences in minutes/day) depending on accelerometer filter (normal vs. low-frequency extension), epoch length (15s vs 60s), and cut-point algorithm (Freedson VM3, Evenson, Troiano) even within the same device (ActiGraph). This undermines cross-study and cross-trial comparability of any MVPA endpoint.
gaps.action: Require a single pre-specified, locked algorithm/filter/epoch/cut-point combination for any trial using MVPA as an endpoint; do not pool or compare MVPA data across studies using different processing pipelines.

gaps.description: Cross-device concordance between activPAL (thigh) and ActiGraph (hip) for MVPA is only weak-to-moderate (CCC 0.20-0.53) and highly dependent on which ActiGraph cut-point is used as the reference; no activPAL method achieved individual-level validated equivalence.
gaps.action: Treat activPAL-derived MVPA as supportive/exploratory only until individual-level equivalence is established; specify device and algorithm explicitly in any endpoint definition.

gaps.description: No record in this corpus establishes a minimal important difference (MID) or minimal clinically important difference (MICD) for MVPA minutes/day in COPD; several RCTs report statistically significant MVPA changes (e.g., PMID:41843815, PMID:41956174) without linking these to an established clinically meaningful threshold.
gaps.action: Seek or commission an MID/MICD estimation study for MVPA in COPD before recommending MVPA as a primary trial endpoint.

gaps.description: No FDA or EMA guidance document is present in this corpus explicitly naming MVPA as a qualified or accepted COPD trial endpoint; existing use across industry-sponsored trials (NCT03357341, NCT05655832) treats MVPA-related measures as secondary/exploratory only.
gaps.action: Conduct a fresh regulatory guidance search (FDA/EMA COPD-specific and cross-indication digital endpoint guidance) rather than relying on this literature corpus, which does not contain primary regulatory sources.

gaps.description: Several devices/platforms (Vivalink, Fitbit, Garmin Vivofit 2, Sensewear) appear in only 1 study each within this corpus, limiting confidence in their evidence base for MVPA measurement specifically in COPD.
gaps.action: Treat these as Tier 2/3 monitor-only candidates; seek additional independent validation studies before considering for trial use.

evidence.clinical_definition: Moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA) refers to time spent in activities ≥3 metabolic equivalents (METs), typically operationalized via accelerometer vector-magnitude or step-cadence cut-points. In COPD, MVPA is reduced relative to healthy controls, declines further with disease severity and after exacerbations, and is a modifiable treatable trait associated with mortality, exacerbation risk, and health status.

gate_rationale.disease_relevance: MVPA is consistently and independently associated with COPD incidence, mortality, exacerbation risk, and health status across multiple large cohorts and RCTs (PMID:41944057, PMID:41174666, PMID:39515216, PMID:38226063, PMID:39219563).
gate_rationale.regulatory_precedent: MVPA/step count has been used as a secondary/exploratory endpoint in multiple industry-sponsored trials (NCT03357341, NCT05655832) and academic RCTs, but no cited record shows FDA/EMA guidance explicitly naming MVPA as a qualified primary COPD endpoint; use is consistently secondary or exploratory.
gate_rationale.sensor_maturity: Multiple validated accelerometer/inertial platforms exist (ActiGraph, activPAL, Axivity, wrist and thigh-worn systems), but cross-device agreement is poor (PMID:42419359, PMID:38394032, PMID:37629381), and algorithm/cutpoint choice materially changes MVPA estimates, indicating measurement standardization is not yet mature.

measurement_spec.primary_metric: Minutes/day in moderate-to-vigorous physical activity (MVPA); daily step count as complementary metric
measurement_spec.epoch: 60s most common in COPD literature; 15s epochs also used and produce different MVPA estimates (PMID:37629381, PMID:38394032)
measurement_spec.algorithm: Cut-point dependent: Freedson VM3 (2011), Evenson (2023), Troiano, and Freedson98 cut-points all in use; no consensus algorithm (PMID:42419359, PMID:37629381, PMID:38394032)
measurement_spec.valid_day_rule: Minimum 3-4 valid wear days for reliable MVPA estimation in COPD/respiratory populations (PMID:39445894); ≥22.5h/day wear time threshold used in asthma reliability study
